description: This is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image displaying arrays of regular triangular nano-indentation marks on the microstructure of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels (both Ni-containing and Ni-free variants studied in the work), with a 2 μm scale bar provided for size reference. The indentation rows are deliberately placed across distinct phase regions to measure the nano-hardness of individual phases and combined phase assemblies. As reported in the associated text, the nano-indentation tests were performed at a loading/unloading rate of 500 μN/s up to a maximum load of 10000 μN with a 20 s hold time, with at least 25 repeated measurements per targeted phase region. Phases characterized include γ austenite, α ferrite, B2 intermetallics (stringer bands, micro-sized particles, nano-discs), κ-carbides, and combined phase regions where individual phase hardness cannot be isolated. This figure validates the phase-resolved nano-hardness measurement approach used to quantify the contribution of each phase to the overall mechanical properties of the lightweight steels, supporting the core conclusion that phase composition, morphology, and interatomic bonding strength govern the material's hardness performance.
